content: Python Object Model Decision Rules for JARVIS: (1) Use `is None` to check if value exists—None is singleton, identity check is correct and faster. (2) Use `is _SENTINEL` to distinguish key-not-found from key-exists-with-None—sentinel is unique object you create. (3) Use `==` for comparing IDs/content—always compare values, works regardless of string creation method. (4) Use `id(obj)` for debugging mutations—print memory addresses to trace which object got mutated, not for production logic. (5) Dynamic strings differ from static strings via `is`—f-strings and literals may have same value but different identities, always use `==`.

Deduplication Layer for JARVIS Memory Ingestion: Between the generator's yield (loading current document) and del (unloading from RAM), there must be a middle layer that: (1) Checks if the document content already exists in ChromaDB (JARVIS's main memory/knowledge base). (2) Only stores truly NEW content to avoid duplicates. This 'novelty gate' ensures JARVIS doesn't re-embed and re-store documents it already knows about. Implementation should use content hashing or semantic similarity threshold to detect duplicates.

Semantic Embedding Comparison over Hash Comparison for JARVIS Deduplication: Hash comparison (SHA256) only catches EXACT byte-for-byte duplicates. Semantic comparison using embeddings catches documents with the SAME MEANING but different words. Hybrid approach: (1) Stage 1 - Hash check first (O(1), catches obvious duplicates instantly). (2) Stage 2 - Embedding similarity check (cosine similarity > 0.85 threshold) for semantic duplicates. This prevents storing paraphrased content, summaries of existing docs, or reformatted versions. Use sentence-transformers for real embeddings, ChromaDB's built-in similarity search in production.

JARVIS Control Interface Design Questions: (1) Input modality: chat vs voice vs both — requires speech-to-text (Whisper) and intent parsing. (2) Backend access: How does the control layer call internal functions (generators, orchestrators)? Options: Direct Python imports, REST API, message queue. (3) Model requirements: Need function-calling LLM (GPT-4, Claude, local Llama) to translate natural language commands into structured function calls like send(PipelineConfig(...))

Context managers guarantee resource cleanup via __enter__/__exit__ protocol. Use for: files, DB connections, GPU memory, locks. Class-based for complex state, @contextmanager decorator for simple setup/teardown. Prevents resource exhaustion in long-running JARVIS (file descriptor leaks, connection pool exhaustion, VRAM leaks, deadlocks).

Event loop is single-threaded scheduler for async I/O. await pauses coroutine, lets others run. Use gather() for parallel calls. Never use blocking calls (time.sleep, requests) inside async code — blocks entire loop.
async def returns coroutine object, not result. Must await to run. Forgetting await = code never runs (RuntimeWarning). Each coroutine can only be awaited once — create new coroutine for each call.

JARVIS Learning Architecture Approved: 6-phase structure (Python->Memory->Agents->Orchestration->Specialists->Integration) validated against Brain/Memory/Body pattern. Key principles locked: (1) Single-model-first before specialists. (2) Hardware constraint awareness (48GB VRAM). (3) 2-3x productivity expectation, not 10x. (4) Epistemic control required for multi-model.

Knowledge Checkpoint (Feb 28 2026): Phases 1-3 COMPLETE. Phase 3 verified with 2 Final Boss scripts: v1 (simulated 10-paper API pipeline) and v2 (11 real PDFs, sync vs async comparison). Async pipeline 1.9x faster overall, 9.9x faster on embedding via gather(). Phase 4 Concurrent Patterns starting. Knowledge base has 12 procedural/idea/decision entries.

asyncio.run() is called ONCE at process entry point. Creates loop, runs main coroutine, cancels pending tasks, closes loop. Never call from async code (RuntimeError) or multiple times (kills connections between calls). Use await inside async world instead.

async with uses __aenter__/__aexit__ for non-blocking resource management. Use for DB connections, HTTP sessions, GPU memory, file I/O. @asynccontextmanager decorator for simple cases. Sync 'with' blocks event loop on slow I/O — always use async with for network/disk resources in JARVIS.

Async generators (async def + yield) combine streaming and concurrency. Use async for to consume. Pipeline composition: chain async generators as stages (read -> transform -> embed). Always use try/finally for cleanup inside async generators — fires even on consumer break. Never collect into list (defeats constant-RAM purpose).

Decision: Skip Phase 1.4 (Concurrent Patterns) and 1.5 (Type Safety) as standalone phases. Move directly to Stage 2 Memory Layer. Rationale: gather()+semaphores already demonstrated in Final Boss scripts; TaskGroup/timeouts/cancellation deferred to pre-Stage 3 (Agents) where real use cases exist; Protocol/Pydantic learned on demand during Stage 2.3+. All Stage 2 prerequisites (async I/O, generators, context managers) are met.

Common async confusions: (1) async with is NOT the event loop -- it is a resource lifecycle manager (open/close). asyncio.run() creates the event loop. (2) async def alone = coroutine (one result). async def + yield = async generator (streams). (3) async for is READ-ONLY consumption -- does NOT use send(). send() is the Phase 2 sync generator protocol. (4) gather() launches concurrent tasks -- it does not embed or store, those happen downstream.

3 architectural gaps in JARVIS vision: (1) No error recovery model — each subsystem needs fallback (Whisper->text, Router uncertain->generalist, Specialist low-confidence->Brain). Design with Result[T,Error] pattern. Address in Stage 1.5 Type Safety. (2) No feedback loop — memory needs pruning, preference learning, relevance decay. Memory is not append-only forever, needs garbage collector. Address in Stage 2. (3) No latency budget — define Fast(<3s, Brain only), Medium(<10s, one specialist), Slow(<45s, multi-specialist with streaming partial results). Router classifies tier first, sets user expectations. Address in Stage 4.

Embeddings are fixed-length float arrays (384-dim for MiniLM) representing text meaning. Cosine similarity measures direction alignment (not magnitude) between vectors. Start with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384-dim, 80MB, CPU, free, private). Must chunk documents (~500 words) before embedding -- long text loses specificity. RAG pipeline: embed query -> cosine search ChromaDB -> inject top-k chunks into LLM prompt. Gotcha: cross-domain style similarity can fool embeddings, use metadata filtering.

VectorDB alternatives reviewed: (1) Massive Context + KV Cache = RED for local JARVIS (Llama 70B + 128K context = ~40GB VRAM, no room), valid only for cloud APIs in Stage 6. (2) GraphRAG = GREEN but premature, scheduled for Stage 4 (needs Agent framework for entity extraction). (3) ColBERT/late interaction = GREEN, added to Stage 2.5. Decision: Build ChromaDB as v1 memory in Stage 2, upgrade to GraphRAG in Stage 4, add optional context caching in Stage 6.

INFERENCE & VRAM GAPS (Stage 4): (1) Speculative Decoding — run tiny draft model (Llama 3B) to generate 8 candidate tokens, 70B verifier checks all 8 in one forward pass, accepts correct prefix. Result: 2-3x free speedup. Fits Stage 4 multi-model architecture (small+large already planned). (2) Mixture of Experts (MoE) — models like Mixtral 8x7B have 46B total params but activate only 12B per token (2 of 8 experts). Get 70B-class quality at 12B VRAM cost (~15GB vs ~35GB). Frees 20GB for embeddings/agents/vectorDB. Fits Stage 4.1 model selection. (3) Quantization (CRITICAL prerequisite) — AWQ 4-bit compresses 70B to 35GB with ~0.3% quality loss, 1.3x faster. GPTQ 4-bit similar at ~0.5% loss. GGUF Q4_K_M allows CPU+GPU split at 40GB, ~0.8% loss. Without quantization JARVIS literally cannot run 70B on 48GB VRAM. FP16 70B = 140GB. Must be covered in Stage 4.1 Local Model Loading.

AGENT RELIABILITY GAPS (Stage 3): (1) Structured Generation / Constrained Decoding — libraries like 'outlines' or 'guidance' force LLM output to conform to a JSON schema or grammar. Model literally cannot produce invalid JSON or call non-existent tools. Without this, tool-calling agents break randomly on malformed output. Example: outlines.generate.json(model, ToolCallSchema) guarantees valid ToolCall every time. Fits Stage 3 (Agent Framework) as tool-calling reliability layer. (2) MemGPT Self-Editing Memory — paper 2310.08560 (already in Research Papers folder). Treats memory as virtual memory hierarchy: Hot (current conversation context), Warm (frequently accessed facts pinned in ChromaDB), Cold (archived on disk). Agent self-manages: promotes important facts cold->warm, evicts stale entries, compresses verbose memories. Production JARVIS cannot rely on manual /memory commands. Fits Stage 3.5 (Memory-Augmented Agents), connect to owned paper.

EVALUATION & OBSERVABILITY GAP (Stage 2.5 / Stage 3): No measurement infrastructure in current roadmap to verify RAG quality or agent correctness. (1) RAGAS — automated RAG evaluation framework. Scores: Faithfulness (is answer grounded in retrieved context?), Relevance (are retrieved chunks relevant to query?), Context Recall (did retrieval find all needed info?), Correctness (is answer factually right?). Run after every pipeline change to catch regressions. (2) LLM-as-Judge — use one LLM to grade another's outputs against ground truth. Cheaper than human eval, correlates well with human judgment for factual tasks. (3) Tracing — tools like Langfuse or Phoenix log every LLM call, retrieval query, tool invocation, latency, and cost. Essential for debugging 'why did JARVIS give a bad answer?' without guessing. Without evaluation: you cannot improve what you cannot measure. Partially fits existing Stage 2.5.5 Evaluation Metrics lesson, but needs expansion into Stage 3 agent evaluation.

sentence-transformers pipeline: Tokenizer (30,522 vocab) -> 6-layer Transformer (22M params) -> Mean Pooling (N token vectors -> 1 sentence vector) -> L2 Normalize. all-MiniLM-L6-v2: 384-dim, 80MB, 256 max tokens. CRITICAL: silently truncates at 256 tokens (~200 words) with no error/warning — always chunk before embedding. Same model MUST be used for ingestion AND query (different models = different vector spaces = broken similarity). When normalized, dot product = cosine (ChromaDB uses this for speed). Load model ONCE at startup, never per-query. Batch encode (batch_size=32) for 3x speedup. Only upgrade from 384-dim when retrieval quality is proven bottleneck (384->768 gives ~1.5% quality gain but doubles index size).

WordPiece tokenization splits rare words into known subword pieces. ## prefix = continuation of previous word (e.g., asynchronous -> 5 tokens: as + ##yn + ##ch + ##ron + ##ous). Both BERT and MiniLM use identical WordPiece tokenizer (30,522 vocab). BERT: 12 layers, 768-dim, 110M params, 512 max tokens. MiniLM: 6 layers, 384-dim, 22.7M params, 256 max tokens. Quality: MiniLM = 97% of BERT at 20% cost. Positional embeddings are a learned lookup table (trained via backprop) mapping position index -> 384-dim vector. Table has fixed rows -- position 257+ does not exist in MiniLM. Silent truncation: tokenizer discards tokens beyond position 255 BEFORE they enter the model -- no error, no warning, content simply does not exist in the embedding.

Gap: jargon_gap + abstraction_gap + missing_prerequisite. User has strong ML/DL math foundation (backprop, gradient descent, loss functions, linear algebra) but ZERO LLM-specific knowledge (tokenization, positional encoding, attention, transformer input pipeline). Key confusion pattern: invisible operations -- user struggles when system performs actions silently (truncation with no error, implicit subword splitting, hidden padding). Does NOT struggle with math itself, struggles with unexplained mechanics. DIRECTIVE: When explaining LLM/transformer internals, (1) never use NLP jargon without immediate definition, (2) use step-by-step mechanical walkthroughs showing exactly what happens at each stage with real numbers/IDs, (3) anchor new concepts to comparison tables against known references (BERT->MiniLM pattern worked), (4) always include verify-it-yourself code blocks.

Transformer architecture complete breakdown: (1) Attention = Q,K,V are 3 learned projections of input. Scores = softmax(Q*K^T/sqrt(d))*V gives weighted sum of all tokens values. (2) Multi-Head splits dim into N parallel heads (MiniLM: 6 heads x 64d) learning different relationship types, concatenated back. (3) FFN = two linear layers with GELU (384->1536->384), adds non-linearity. (4) LayerNorm normalizes per-token across features (not batch). Residual connections prevent gradient vanishing. (5) Encoder (BERT/MiniLM) sees ALL tokens bidirectionally for embeddings. Decoder (GPT/Llama) masks future tokens for generation. (6) MiniLM vs Llama: same Q,K,V math, Llama adds RoPE (rotary position), GQA (shared K,V), Gated FFN, RMSNorm -- all scale optimizations, not fundamental changes.

Gap: jargon_gap + connection_gap. User saw Q,K,V and FFN labels in parameter table (Q4) but had no idea what they meant. Knew matrix multiplication and backprop but lacked the vocabulary and the connection between parameter table entries and their computational roles. Pattern confirms prior profile: user understands math operations but not domain-specific component names. DIRECTIVE: When referencing transformer components in future explanations, always expand abbreviations on first use (FFN = Feed-Forward Network) and show the tensor shapes to connect to known linear algebra. User responds well to architectural flow diagrams (input -> layer -> output) with concrete shapes at each step.

Complete transformer walkthrough from Attention Is All You Need (2017). Paper arch: N=6, d_model=512, h=8, d_k=d_v=64, d_ff=2048. Input = token_embed + position_embed → (n, d_model). Attention = softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V; scaling exists because dot product variance = d_k, must normalize to 1. Multi-head: split into h parallel attentions, concat, output project. FFN: max(0, xW1+b1)W2+b2 (expand 4x then compress). LayerNorm: gamma * ((x-mu)/(sigma+eps)) + beta; gamma/beta are learned (init 1/0, trained via backprop same as W_q). Residual: x+f(x), gradient includes +1 term preventing vanishing. All sublayers must output d_model to allow addition. Training: Adam (beta2=0.98), warmup 4k steps, dropout 0.1, label smoothing 0.1. Base model: 8xP100 12h.

Gap: abstraction_gap + scale_confusion. User understood Q5 high-level architecture but could not connect shapes to actual numbers. Specific failures: (1) did not understand where input matrix comes from -- needed explicit token_embed + position_embed walkthrough, (2) could not visualize why W_q is (384x384) -- needed to see 4x4 example with hand-computed multiplications, (3) gamma/beta in LayerNorm meant nothing -- needed init values, training process, and concrete output calculation, (4) residual connection was a formula, not a mechanism -- needed gradient math showing the +1 term. DIRECTIVE: This user CANNOT learn from shape-only diagrams. Every new formula must include (a) actual numbers filled in, (b) hand-computed intermediate steps, (c) what each variable is initialized to before training, (d) what changes it during training (backprop). Shape-level descriptions are table-of-contents, not explanations.

CURIOSITY MODEL: User generates questions via 3 triggers: (1) UNDEFINED TERM -- any abbreviation, symbol, or jargon used without definition triggers immediate clarification (##, Q/K/V, FFN, gamma/beta all triggered). (2) SHAPE WITHOUT NUMBERS -- showing tensor shapes like (8,384)x(384,8)=(8,8) is NOT an explanation to this user, it is a table of contents. They need actual numbers filled in with hand-computed intermediate steps. (3) INVISIBLE OPERATIONS -- any system behavior that happens silently (truncation, implicit splitting, hidden padding) causes anxiety and confusion. User also has a strong ORIGIN TRACING pattern: for every variable, they need to know where it starts (init value), what changes it (backprop), and what it converges to. DIRECTIVE: Before introducing ANY new formula, must provide: (a) define every symbol, (b) show init values, (c) fill in real numbers, (d) compute step-by-step, (e) explain what changes these numbers during training.

TEACHING EFFECTIVENESS RANKING (high to low): [RANK 1] Hand-computed numerical examples with every intermediate step shown -- ONLY thing that resolves abstraction_gap. [RANK 2] Comparison tables anchoring unknown to known (BERT-vs-MiniLM pattern). [RANK 3] Step-by-step pipeline flow diagrams with shapes at each stage. [RANK 4] Verify-it-yourself code blocks. [RANK 5] Summary tables (accepted ONLY after detailed version understood). [FAILS] Shape-only notation -- user explicitly rejected as high-level view. [FAILS] Jargon-first explanation -- every undefined term triggers follow-up. [FAILS] Just-trust-me assertions -- user demands WHY behind every design choice. DIRECTIVE: Never use shape-only shorthand as primary explanation. Always lead with numbers, follow with shapes as summary.

META-PATTERN: User is an exhaustive due-diligence learner building a personalized AI. Key traits: (1) COMPLETENESS DRIVE -- refuses to move forward with partial understanding. (2) BUILDER MINDSET -- every concept must connect to JARVIS production, never academic interest. (3) METACOGNITIVE AWARENESS -- actively designs systems to capture own learning patterns (designed cognitive profiling workflow themselves). (4) FOUNDATION STRENGTH -- strong ML/DL math (backprop, gradients, linear algebra, Xavier init, ResNets) means transformer concepts CAN be explained via tensor ops they already know. (5) LEARNING VELOCITY -- once concept clicks with proper explanation, zero repeated questions. Each clarification is a NEW gap not a re-explanation. DIRECTIVE: This user is a fast learner blocked only by explanation quality. If they ask a follow-up, the failure is in the explanation, not their comprehension. Upgrade explanation depth, do not simplify.

PAD tokens do NOT contaminate embeddings. attention_mask injects -infinity before softmax, making PAD contribution exactly 0.000. Mean pooling also uses attention_mask to exclude PAD vectors. Padding wastes GPU FLOPS (token embed lookup, Q/K/V projection, attention computation, FFN for PAD positions x6 layers) but produces mathematically identical final embeddings regardless of padding amount.

Token Embedding Table (30522x384) and Position Embedding Table (512x384) are static 2D arrays in VRAM. Lookup is O(1) array indexing not computation. Framework silently generates position_ids tensor [0,1,2...N] matching input_ids shape. Results element-wise added to produce (batch, seq, 384). Position table is 512 rows (BERT inheritance) but sentence-transformers caps at 256 because Self-Attention scales O(N^2).

sentence-transformers model.encode() internally sorts all input strings by estimated token length before creating batches. Short strings batched together, long strings together. Minimizes intra-batch padding waste automatically. Results un-sorted back to original order before returning.

Gap: abstraction_gap + connection_gap. User understands token IDs, tokenization, batch shapes individually but could not visualize embedding tables as physical memory arrays. DIRECTIVE: When explaining lookup-based mechanisms, draw real-sized table with Row Index | Dim columns, reveal invisible ops like position_ids auto-generation, prove purpose by computing dot product WITH vs WITHOUT.

Gap: abstraction_gap. User understands transformer PIPELINE but internal MATH of attention (Q/K/V multiplications, softmax scaling, FFN layers) remains a black box. User explicitly acknowledges this gap. DIRECTIVE: Do not force matrix math. Accept boundary for now. Revisit when transformer math becomes a build blocker.

JARVIS Modality Router: text -> chunk -> MiniLM -> ChromaDB. Audio -> Whisper -> text -> MiniLM. Video -> audio -> Whisper + keyframes -> CLIP/SigLIP. Images -> CLIP/SigLIP. All converge to vectors. MiniLM is Phase 1 text-only. Avoid multimodal-in-one models (ImageBind).

Three chunking strategies: (1) Fixed-size ~200 words with ~50 overlap -- recommended starting point. (2) Semantic chunking on paragraph boundaries. (3) Sentence-level -- TOO GRANULAR. Strategy 1 recommended. Sentence-level best within Strategy 1 boundaries.

FINE-TUNING FAILURE DIAGNOSTIC MAP - When user hits a training failure, match symptoms to math prerequisites. (1) SYMPTOM: Loss not decreasing after N steps. STUDY: Loss functions (cross-entropy mechanics, gradient computation). FOCUS: What each loss value physically means, why plateau happens. (2) SYMPTOM: Model produces gibberish/repetitive text after fine-tuning. STUDY: Gradient norms through attention layers, learning rate warmup/decay schedules. FOCUS: Why large gradients destroy pretrained weights. (3) SYMPTOM: Fine-tuned model forgets base knowledge (catastrophic forgetting). STUDY: Weight update propagation through transformer layers, LoRA rank selection math. FOCUS: Why targeting specific weight matrices (Q,K,V,FFN) matters - each matrix controls different behavior. (4) SYMPTOM: LoRA fine-tune converges but quality is mediocre. STUDY: Which weight matrices to target (Q and V matrices control attention patterns, FFN controls knowledge storage). FOCUS: Rank selection - too low loses expressiveness, too high overfits. (5) SYMPTOM: Training loss drops but validation loss increases (overfitting). STUDY: Regularization math (dropout mechanics inside attention, weight decay). FOCUS: How dropout randomly zeros attention connections during training. (6) SYMPTOM: Need custom loss function for domain-specific behavior. STUDY: Backpropagation through softmax and attention, chain rule through QxKT/sqrt(d_k). FOCUS: How gradients flow from loss backward through each transformer layer. (7) SYMPTOM: Model quality degrades on long inputs after fine-tuning. STUDY: Position embedding interpolation, RoPE rotation math. FOCUS: How position encodings scale with sequence length. DIRECTIVE: When user encounters any of these symptoms, identify the matching entry, prescribe the specific STUDY topic and FOCUS area, then teach using the /learn workflow with concrete numerical examples from their actual training logs.

Cosine similarity = dot product of two L2-normalized vectors. Range [-1, 1]. Value of 1.0 = identical direction (same meaning), 0.0 = orthogonal (unrelated), -1.0 = opposite. Raw dot product is unreliable for similarity because it is biased by vector magnitude — a long vector scores high against everything regardless of meaning. Cosine similarity eliminates magnitude bias by dividing by both norms. sentence-transformers L2-normalizes all output vectors, so cosine similarity and dot product produce identical rankings when using normalized vectors. For JARVIS retrieval: cosine similarity is the default metric in ChromaDB.

Embedding dimensions = length of the float array representing meaning. MiniLM produces 384-dim vectors. BERT-base produces 768-dim. Larger models (e.g., e5-large) produce 1024-dim. Tradeoffs: (1) STORAGE: 384 floats x 4 bytes = 1.5KB per vector. 768-dim = 3KB. At 1M documents, 384-dim = 1.5GB VRAM, 768-dim = 3GB. (2) SEARCH SPEED: Cosine similarity is O(d) where d = dimensions. 384-dim is 2x faster than 768-dim per comparison. (3) QUALITY: Higher dimensions capture finer semantic distinctions but with diminishing returns past ~768. 384-dim MiniLM achieves 95% of BERT-768 quality on standard benchmarks at 50% the compute and storage cost. For JARVIS Phase 1: 384-dim MiniLM is optimal — fits on RTX 3090 with room for Llama, fast enough for real-time retrieval, quality sufficient for RAG.

DECISION: MiniLM (384-dim) is Phase 1-2 scaffolding ONLY. JARVIS endgame requires domain-specific embedding models per specialist: CodeBERT/StarEncoder for code, PubMedBERT for medical, SPECTER2 for scientific papers, plus ColBERT for token-level retrieval. The retrieval pipeline architecture (chunk -> embed -> store -> retrieve) is model-agnostic by design. Swapping embedding models requires re-embedding all documents in that collection but ZERO code changes to the pipeline. ChromaDB collections are per-domain, each using its optimal embedding model. MiniLM handles general text, conversation history, and user preferences permanently.

JARVIS COMPLETE SPECIALIST ROSTER (12 experts): (1) Orchestrator - Llama 70B Q4 local, intent routing. (2) Engineer - DeepSeek-Coder, software/pipelines. (3) Scientist - ArXiv fine-tune, physics/optics/LaTeX. (4) Doctor - BioMistral/Med42, medical/pharma. (5) Operator - OpenVLA, robotics/hardware control. (6) Electrician - IEEE fine-tune, circuit design/EM theory/signal processing/PCB/antenna. (7) Mechanic - engineering handbooks fine-tune, CAD/FEA/thermal/structural/materials. (8) Chemist - PubChem fine-tune, material properties/reactions/nanotechnology/metallurgy. (9) Strategist - long-context LLM, patent analysis/IP/competitive research. (10) Analyst - base LLM, financial modeling/market analysis. (11) Guardian - SecureFalcon/CodeLlama, cybersecurity/vulnerability analysis. (12) Interface - Whisper V3 + Qwen-VL, voice/vision I/O.

JARVIS EMBEDDING CLUSTER MAP (6 unique models serving 12 specialists): Cluster A: MiniLM 384-dim (general text, conversation, user prefs, Strategist patents, Analyst financial). Cluster B: SPECTER2 768-dim (Scientist ArXiv, Electrician IEEE, Mechanic engineering papers). Cluster C: CodeBERT 768-dim (Engineer software, Guardian security/exploits). Cluster D: PubMedBERT 768-dim (Doctor medical, Chemist partial overlap). Cluster E: CLIP/SigLIP 512-dim (Operator robotics vision, Interface images). Cluster F: MatSciBERT 768-dim (Mechanic materials, Chemist compounds). VRAM: All 6 embedding models ~2GB total, fit permanently alongside Orchestrator. Specialist LLMs swap dynamically (4-8GB each, 10-20s swap time) or run on ephemeral cloud GPUs.

TurboQuant (arXiv 2504.19874, Google Research) — KV cache vector quantization achieving quality neutrality at 3.5 bits/channel (4.5x compression vs 16-bit). Two-stage: (1) random rotation + optimal scalar quantization per coordinate for MSE, (2) 1-bit QJL on residual for unbiased inner products. Data-oblivious (no calibration needed), online-applicable. Two JARVIS use cases: (1) KV CACHE: 5x longer context on same VRAM — Llama 70B on A100 goes from ~20K to ~100K+ token context. Implementation via vLLM/SGLang flags, not custom code. Phase 4+. (2) VECTOR DB: Outperforms standard Product Quantization for nearest neighbor search with zero indexing time. 1M docs at 384-dim: 1.5GB -> ~96MB at 2-bit. Phase 2.5 when studying NN search optimization.

ChromaDB Organization: Use COLLECTIONS (hard boundaries) when embedding models differ (SPECTER2 vs CodeBERT = incompatible vector spaces). Use METADATA FILTERS (soft boundaries) when same embedding model but different domains (Python vs Rust code). Metadata filtering executes BEFORE vector similarity via SQLite, drastically reducing dot product count. Operators: $eq, $ne, $gt, $lt, $in, $and, $or.

PATTERN: forward_projection. User does not learn tools -- they validate that tools fit the JARVIS endgame. When learning ChromaDB basics (5-doc demo), immediately asked about domain-level filtering for 12 specialists. Skips tutorial mindset entirely. Thinks in systems not tools. Sees Orchestrator routing before being told. DIRECTIVE: Lead with endgame architecture placement FIRST, then API details. User has architectural intuition -- they need confirmation and implementation specifics, not motivation.

META-DECISION: knowledge_base.jsonl schema must EVOLVE beyond static gap-tracking. Cognitive profile is foundational dataset for JARVIS to model its creator. New dimensions: (1) Expectation signals -- what user expects systems to do before being told. (2) Identity signals -- who user IS as a builder (specialists not generalists, production not prototypes). (3) Goal alignment -- how each step connects to endgame. (4) Decision velocity -- how fast user makes architectural decisions. New tags: forward_projection, architectural_intuition, production_mindset, endgame_alignment, identity_signal, decision_velocity, expectation_pattern. Every prompt evaluated for NEW cognitive signal.

ChromaDB SQLite Debugging Sequence: When JARVIS fails to retrieve a known fact, audit the chroma.sqlite3 file in this exact order: (1) collections table: Verify the target namespace exists and dimension matches the embedding model. (2) embeddings_queue table: The source of truth. Check if the raw text/JSON actually made it to the disk firehose. (3) embedding_metadata: The SQLite filter engine. Verify the JSON keys from the queue were properly exploded into SQL columns for the where= clause. If a row is missing here, the pre-filter will silently drop it before vector math. (4) embedding_fulltext_search_content: The FTS5 fallback engine (keyword search).

PATTERN: runbook_mindset + system_accountability. (1) User asks for the sequence of components to evaluate, mapping directly to a troubleshooting mindset. They want the execution path for debugging, not just a static map of components. (2) User holds the system strictly accountable to newly defined protocols. When informed no new cognitive signal was present, user immediately challenged it based on the meta-evolution rules defined just one prompt prior. DIRECTIVE: Every explanation of a black-box component (like a DB) MUST include the sequential debugging path (how to trace a failure through its internals). Furthermore, consistently apply evolving cognitive metrics (expectation_pattern, endgame_alignment) to every single prompt — do not default to empty static profiling.

OVERRIDE: User rejects repetitive enforcement. Do not wait for manual prompts to execute critical baseline functions (like cognitive profiling). If a manual correction happens more than once, instantly convert it into a systemic workspace rule. Furthermore, user values self-auditing architectures (e.g., forcing the AI to evaluate if its own schemas are stale before writing to them).

JARVIS Model Taxonomy: (1) Embedding Models (SPECTER2, MiniLM) live in Memory Layer to vectorise text. Cannot chat. (2) Open-Weight LLMs (Llama, DeepSeek) live in Brain/Experts for local, private generation. (3) Cloud APIs (Claude, Gemini, GPT) are external, closed models used only as internet-connected fallback tools, not core local architecture.

Gap: taxonomy_confusion. User viewed all AI names (Claude, Llama, SPECTER2) as interchangeable chat models. DIRECTIVE: Always rigidly define a models architectural class (Embedding vs Generative vs Cloud API) when introducing a new model name. Connect its class directly to a JARVIS component (Memory vs Brain vs External).

API vs Local Economics: Heavy agentic work via API costs $10-$50/day. Chat UI Pro subscriptions ($20/mo) do not grant API access. Local 70B models (quantized) on dual 3090s provide GPT-4-class reasoning for free and must be the primary Orchestrator to ensure privacy and avoid bankruptcy. APIs remain strictly as fallback experts.

Expectation Calibration: Users cannot train 70B models locally due to VRAM (gradient/optimizer overhead). Training from scratch is impossible. The JARVIS Expert strategy relies on downloading domain-specific open open-weight models (DeepSeek-Coder, BioMistral) and applying cheap LoRA fine-tuning, then dynamically swapping them in and out of GPU VRAM one at a time.

PATTERN: architectural_benchmarking. Apollo Gen2 Pipeline Architecture (Celebal Technologies project). End-to-end: Dynamics 365 -> Synapse Link -> ADLS replicate/ (headerless CSVs + model.json) -> Job1 Preprocessing (adds headers, validates) -> ADLS incoming/ -> Job2 DLT (422 pipelines: 211 STG streaming tables + 211 BRZ SCD2 tables). Two-Job Pattern rationale: (1) Different compute needs -- preprocessing = single node, DLT = autoscaling. (2) Independent failure domains -- preprocessing failure does not waste DLT startup. (3) Clean separation -- file ops vs streaming CDC. Scale: 211 entities (206 delta + 5 full load), 422 DLT pipelines, 206 BRZ schema files.

Layer Responsibility Separation (Medallion): STG = raw append only. BRZ = CDC + typing. Silver = business logic. Mixing these makes debugging impossible. Each layer has ONE job. Idempotency by Design: Deleting a checkpoint causes Autoloader to re-read ALL files from scratch -> STG duplicates. BRZ CDC deduplicates by PK naturally; STG append does NOT. Schema-as-Code: One schema definition per entity referenced everywhere. When a column changes, update in ONE place not three. This is the foundation of JARVIS Memory Layer design -- each layer (STG/BRZ/Silver) maps to episodic/semantic/cognitive tiers.

Silver Views over Tables: (1) No storage cost -- Silver does not duplicate Bronze. (2) Always fresh -- reads latest Bronze at query time. (3) No orchestration needed. (4) Materialize only if a Silver view is too slow for Gold reads. Gold Load Strategies: Append (I) = daily snapshots/incremental facts, history preserved. Truncate-and-Load (T) = fully recomputed datasets, entire table rebuilt each run. Scheduling: Gold runs on schedule regardless of upstream state. Silver views enable this -- no Silver job to wait for. _mrg Pattern (Gen1 to Gen2 Translation): Gen1 wrote to _mrg temp table then Control Framework applied load type. Gen2: single write to target Gold with load strategy in YAML. EDP columns backward compat: where edp_is_current AND edp_record_type != D becomes WHERE __end_at IS NULL in Gen2.

BRZ Delta Load CDC (SCD Type 2, No Deletes). Keys: entity PK (usually single, sometimes composite). Sequence by: file modification time (file-level, not row-level). SCD Type 2: full history with __start_at / __end_at. track_history_except: operational metadata columns -- prevents false versions from metadata-only changes. CRITICAL INSIGHT: If a record disappears from subsequent batches, it stays ACTIVE forever (__END_AT = NULL). Delta load SCD2 only shows INSERTs and UPDATEs -- NEVER deletes. This is by design, not a bug. 4-view chain in BRZ pipeline: v_entity_prebrz -> v_entity_validated -> v_entity_filtered -> v_entity_typed -> CDC write. write_source_view parameter points to the LAST view in the chain.

BRZ Full Load CDC (Snapshot SCD Type 2, With Soft Deletes). Keys: Composite (varies per entity). Mechanism: compares consecutive full snapshots. New key -> INSERT. Changed attribute -> close old + INSERT new (SCD2). Absent key -> SOFT DELETE. Reappears -> RE-INSERT. STG count > BRZ count for full loads is EXPECTED, not a bug: if 2 snapshots (118 + 250 rows), STG has 368 (all appended). BRZ active count = 250 (latest snapshot only). Snapshot CDC is processed by py_functions/snapshot_cdc_func.py which processes snapshots SEQUENTIALLY. If a snapshot fails, the next is never processed. CRITICAL: Snapshot CDC is incompatible with Continuous Mode (only triggered mode works). The source function does batch operations (.collect(), spark.sql with MIN/MAX) which cannot run in a streaming loop.

Synapse Link / Dynamics 365 Quirks (all discovered in production): (1) SinkCreatedOn/SinkModifiedOn are NOT Dynamics 365 fields -- Synapse Link metadata, US date format (M/d/yyyy h:mm:ss a). Dynamics uses ISO 8601. These need to_timestamp() not .cast(). (2) All Synapse Link CSVs are headerless. Headers come from model.json (delta) or schemas.json (full load). (3) Full load CSVs always have trailing commas -- every row ends with comma -> phantom empty column. Auto-detect fix: if len(df.columns) == len(expected_columns) + 1: df = df.drop(df.columns[-1]). (4) Full load entities all live in ONE ADLS folder -- must be split into per-entity folders by preprocessing. (5) ADLS folder names are case-sensitive in practice -- Autoloader paths must match exact case. (6) Synapse Link can create folders with leading/trailing spaces -- always .strip() folder name lookups.

MOST DANGEROUS BUG IN PRODUCTION: Silent NULL from .cast('TIMESTAMP') on non-ISO dates. When US date format (M/d/yyyy h:mm:ss a) is cast with .cast('TIMESTAMP'), Spark returns NULL silently. No error, no warning. Pipeline succeeded, data looked complete, but ALL timestamp columns were NULL. Discovered only by manual inspection. FIX: Use to_timestamp(col, 'M/d/yyyy h:mm:ss a') with explicit format pattern. SELECT * EXCEPT(SinkCreatedOn, SinkModifiedOn), to_timestamp(SinkCreatedOn, 'M/d/yyyy h:mm:ss a') AS SinkCreatedOn. RULE: .cast() is safe for ISO 8601 dates but silently destroys non-ISO dates. ALWAYS verify type-cast columns have non-NULL values after first run.

DELTA TYPE WIDENING TRAP: DECIMAL and DOUBLE are incompatible type families in Delta Lake. delta.enableTypeWidening supports: INT->LONG, FLOAT->DOUBLE, DECIMAL(p1,s1)->DECIMAL(p2,s2) where p2>=p1. Does NOT support: DECIMAL->DOUBLE (precision loss) or DOUBLE->DECIMAL (quantization). Scenario: historical load writes Amount as DECIMAL(18,2) via schema transform. Incremental API source returns DOUBLE. Both append to same streaming table. Result: runtime failure. FIX: Align types at source -- add explicit CAST(Amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)) in incremental pipeline before write. RULE: Always cast to match the creator's type when multiple writers target the same table.

Type Casting Chain: Source (Dynamics 365) -> CSV (all text) -> STG (all STRING) -> BRZ (typed). Mechanism 1 - Schema Transform (.cast): for ISO dates, numbers, booleans. Mechanism 2 - SQL Transform (to_timestamp): for US-format dates and column renaming. Schema Enforcement Rule: Enforce at ONE layer only. STG = raw append, BRZ = schema gate. LHP schema transform with enforcement:strict does three things in one step: Filter (drop unlisted columns), Cast (type conversion), Rename (arrow syntax: old_name -> new_name). Full load schemas NOT re-enforced in BRZ because preprocessing already applied correct columns. Enforce once, trust downstream. CSV Autoloader reads EVERYTHING as STRING -- no type promotion.

SCD2 Integrity Tests (mandatory for every SCD2 table): (1) Active row uniqueness: only one __END_AT IS NULL per key. Can silently break if CDC processes overlap. (2) Late arrival detection: __START_AT > __END_AT catches temporal corruption. (3) Cross-batch dedup: catches same key appearing active in multiple processing batches. SCD2 is a WRITE PATTERN, not a guarantee. NULL Aggregation on Empty Sets: SUM(), AVG(), MAX() over zero rows return NULL, not 0. Fix: COALESCE(SUM(...), 0) when WHERE clause can legitimately return zero rows. Test execution is NOT sequential with streaming writes -- a test querying a table being written may see zero rows. Design tests against stable state only, or accept first-run failures.

Apollo SIT Testing Patterns (17 cases total). Key cases: SIT-01 New Record Insert (basic CDC). SIT-02 Standard Update (SCD2 close+open). SIT-03 Absent from Batch (delta load ignores absent -- stays ACTIVE). SIT-04 No-Change Record (track_history_except prevents false versions). SIT-05 Out-of-Order Arrival (sequence_by resolves). SIT-10 Schema Drift (Autoloader addNewColumns). SIT-12 Null PK Drop (DQE expect_all_or_drop). SIT-13 Pipeline Recovery (checkpoint recovery, no duplicates). Warn vs Fail maturity rule: FAIL = data integrity gates (PK, SCD2, CDC). WARN = observability (freshness, schema drift). Start with warn in dev, promote to fail in prod. Do NOT test hard gates -- focus on silent failures. Source Type Compatibility: PK uniqueness per batch only valid for CloudFiles, not API or Materialized Views.

Column name casing creates silent data corruption in Spark. Different sources producing Line_Of_Business vs Line_of_Business become TWO separate columns in a UNION. Spark SQL is case-sensitive for column names in certain contexts. Fix: Establish a canonical column name registry. Enforce at the earliest ingestion point. Schema metadata queries are also case-sensitive: information_schema.columns stores table names in lowercase. Querying with original casing returns zero rows. Always LOWER() on both sides. Real incident: LRC project had Cost_Centre (API source) vs CostCentre (BYOD source) causing UNRESOLVED_COLUMN. Fix: standardize to CostCentre across ALL layers at the earliest point.

Decision: Fail Hard, NOT route to Unprocessed folder. Unprocessed folders are where data goes to die. Nobody monitors them. Pipeline failure triggers alerts. raise ValueError('specific message') beats silent routing every time. Best Practices Summary from Apollo production: (1) Browse raw data before building pipelines -- 30 min saves days of debugging. (2) Silent failures are worse than loud failures. (3) Enforce schema once at the boundary. (4) Every row needs operational metadata. (5) Never edit auto-generated files -- edit the source config and regenerate. (6) Batch-generate at scale -- Python scripts for 200+ config files. (7) Test with small data first, then scale. (8) Document the type mapping chain: Source -> CSV -> Spark -> STG -> BRZ.

Decision: Separate DQE Expectations File. Same 3 expectations apply to all 206 delta entities. One JSON file, referenced by all. Change once, applies everywhere. Decision: Generated Code is an Artifact, NOT a Source of Truth. Editing generated files is always overwritten on next generation. Persistent changes must go in the SOURCE configuration. In code-generation architectures, identify the generation boundary and never make manual changes below it. Notebook generation best practice: generate .ipynb via Python scripts, not heredocs (JSON quoting breaks). Use re.sub with a mapping function for renumbering, not sequential replace (cascading bug).

Serverless Compute Is NOT Infinite Parallelism. Serverless DLT starts small and autoscales -- does not give 200 slots immediately. Running multiple large pipelines simultaneously SPLITS available capacity. Sequential execution often finishes faster than parallel on serverless. Orchestration order matters even with serverless. POC Runtime: 4-job sequential total ~33m Run1 / ~30m Run2. Counter-intuitive finding: Snapshot-CDC BRZ pipelines got faster on rerun (14m->3m42s) but STG and unified-CDC pipelines got SLOWER. Net improvement comes from BRZ wins outweighing STG regressions. SLA trade-off: sequential 4-job ~2x slower than consolidated. Running 4 jobs in parallel bounds by longest child (~14min).

Redeployment Safety in Databricks DLT: Clean slate = delete pipelines + drop tables + redeploy. If you only delete pipelines, tables survive. New pipelines re-read all files -> DUPLICATES at STG. Re-running preprocessing without cleanup overwrites incoming/ files -> Autoloader sees them as new -> STG duplicates. BRZ CDC deduplicates so BRZ is fine, but STG is dirty. Stop continuous pipelines BEFORE redeployment -- running pipeline uses old code after deploy. Developer Isolation: table name suffixes must propagate through EVERY layer: YAML, SQL, Python, and infrastructure config. Missing suffix in ONE place causes TABLE_NOT_FOUND or TABLE_ALREADY_EXISTS. Databricks CLI Gotchas: (1) pipelines delete uses positional args not flags. (2) list-pipelines outputs UTF-16 on Windows. (3) max_retries: 0 is per-task, add to each task individually. (4) databricks bundle deploy --force-lock needed when previous deploy did not clean up.

Per-Environment Config Files: one config file per environment (dev/test/preprod/prod). Each file self-contained -- no inheritance, no conditional logic. Duplication in config is cheaper than debugging a wrong-environment override at 2 AM. File Path Casing Breaks on Linux CI/CD: Windows case-insensitive (Peoplesoft/ = peoplesoft/). Linux case-sensitive (Peoplesoft/ != peoplesoft/). Solution: lowercase_snake_case for ALL directory and file names. If developing on Windows but deploying to Linux, treat casing as a potential breaking change. OAuth Refresh Tokens are Dynamic Secrets: tokens get overwritten on each API call. Static secret -> stale by next pipeline run. Fix: write new refresh token back to secret store after each successful auth exchange. Never store credentials in plaintext config -- mandatory gate before promoting code beyond dev.

LRC & PeopleSoft Gen2 Operational Metadata Standard (two tiers by source type): LRC API (custom_datasource, no files): operational_metadata = [_processing_timestamp] only. Everything else (CloudFiles -- CSV, JSON, PSV): [_source_file_path, _source_file_modification_time, _processing_timestamp]. _source_file_name NOT used -- _source_file_path covers it and is more useful for debugging. CRITICAL: Every column in track_history_except must ACTUALLY EXIST in data flowing into CDC. Listing non-existent columns will not error, but OMITTING existing metadata columns causes false SCD2 versions on EVERY run. CDC sequence_by for API sources: use _processing_timestamp (added by LHP at extraction view level). sequence_by (ordering) and track_history_except (change detection) are INDEPENDENT concepts in DLT CDC.

DLT REFERENCE_DLT_DATASET_OUTSIDE_QUERY_DEFINITION error: Referencing a pipeline dataset outside a dataset query definition (@dlt.table annotation) is not supported. WHEN it fails: if you unify STG and BRZ into one pipeline, spark.sql inside a Python callback (snapshot source_function) hits this error because the STG table is now an in-pipeline dataset. Cross-pipeline reads (STG in one pipeline, BRZ in another) are external UC table reads -- spark.sql handles these normally. Same-Pipeline STG+BRZ compatibility matrix: Regular CDC (mode: cdc, apply_changes) with delta source -> YES works. Snapshot CDC with source_table -> YES works. Snapshot CDC with source_function (Python callback with spark.sql) -> NO, fails. Keep split across 2 pipelines. Decision rule: if BRZ template injects a Python function as source, STG and BRZ MUST live in separate pipelines with depends_on.

Gen1 BRZ SCD2 Migration Problem -- Double SCD2 Breaks Active Records. Scenario: Migrating from Gen1 BRZ (already SCD2) to Gen2 BRZ (also SCD2). When Gen1 BRZ Parquet is exported as-is and loaded through Gen2 STG -> BRZ CDC, 100% of active rows end up with WRONG business values despite row counts matching perfectly. Why: Gen1 BRZ has multiple versions per PK with edp_is_current/edp_effective_dttm. Gen2 CDC picks ONE winner per PK based on sequence_by. But edp_effective_dttm is IDENTICAL for all versions. edp_record_insert_dttm on the EXPIRED version is often the LATEST -> CDC picks the wrong (expired) row. Evidence: ITEM_TRANS -- 628/628 ACCOUNT mismatches (100%), 623/628 MONETARY_AMOUNT sign flips. Fix Option 1: Filter historical load to WHERE edp_is_current = True -- 1 row per PK, preserves correct data, loses SCD2 history. LESSON: Running CDC on data that is already SCD2 creates double-SCD2 which sequence_by alone cannot resolve.

Databricks Master Job Orchestration via run_job_task pattern. Pattern: chain multiple independent jobs into one master schedule. Useful when splitting a large job into domain-specific jobs (stg_brz_ps, stg_brz_lrc_byod, brz_slv_finance) but needing cross-domain ordering (all STG+BRZ jobs complete before SLV). In YAML: tasks use run_job_task with job_id referencing ${resources.jobs.JOB_NAME.id}. Properties: child jobs remain independently runnable. depends_on expresses cross-job ordering. Tasks without depends_on run in parallel. run_job_task is a Databricks-native task type, no custom Python needed. LHP Limitation: master YAML cannot be generated by LHP -- create and maintain manually in resources/. Row Count Test fix: use custom_sql with WHERE _processing_timestamp >= (SELECT MAX(_processing_timestamp) - INTERVAL 5 MINUTES FROM table) to isolate exactly one batch when testing append-only STG tables.

Delta allows multi-writer streaming tables via append_flow. Pattern: Historical (once=True batch Parquet append_flow) + Incremental (streaming append_flow) -> same target table. Exactly ONE writer must own table creation (create_table: true). Others only append. If nothing creates the table (historical flow removed), LHP validation fails: LHP-VAL-009 no creator. Move create_table: true to the incremental writer (always runs) to avoid breakage when flowgroups are toggled. _source_file_path NULL% in mixed STG tables is EXPECTED: historical rows (read_files SQL) have NULL _source_file_path, incremental (Autoloader) are populated. NULL% = historical_rows / total_rows. At 99.9% historical, 99.9% NULL is correct. GOTCHA: if _source_file_path is in track_history_except and pipeline runs with ONLY historical data, BRZ CDC fails with UNRESOLVED_COLUMN -- because STG was created by historical flow which does not add that column.

PeopleSoft Snapshot Function TIMESTAMP Cast Bug (resolved 2026-04). Original code used bare string comparison in Spark SQL: WHERE _source_file_modification_time > '{latest_snapshot_version}'. Fixed to: WHERE _source_file_modification_time > TIMESTAMP '{latest_snapshot_version}'. Without explicit TIMESTAMP cast, Spark's string-to-timestamp comparison is unreliable depending on the string representation of the Python object. Both comparison lines needed the fix. LESSON: ALWAYS use TIMESTAMP 'literal' when injecting a Python timestamp value into a Spark SQL string. Never rely on implicit string->timestamp comparison in Spark SQL. This applies to any Python f-string interpolation into Spark SQL temporal comparisons.
