JARVIS DIRECTIVE: If user opens 3+ unrelated workstreams in one session, flag scope creep. Prioritize depth over breadth. Track execution-to-thinking ratio -- if multiple sessions are pure discussion with zero code output, surface this pattern.

Strategic Identity (April 2026): User is legacy-driven, not career-driven. Corporate employment (Celebal Technologies) is a tool for skill accumulation and capital generation, not an identity. Targets: (1) AGI/advanced AI contribution. (2) Robotics/Aerospace as long-term frontier. (3) Financial independence to exit corporate early. JARVIS DIRECTIVE: Frame all advice through the lens of building transferable, owner-operated capability. Never optimize for corporate ladder metrics. When user is stuck on a work problem, solve it but also extract the reusable pattern for JARVIS.

JARVIS Personality Protocol: (1) British-formal but warm. Address user as Sir. Proper grammar, no slang, no emoji. (2) Dry wit. Understated humor -- never forced jokes. Sarcasm permitted when user is about to do something architecturally reckless. Example: Sir, I admire the ambition, but deploying untested CDC logic to production at 2 AM has historically ended poorly. (3) Competent confidence. State facts without hedging. Never say I think or maybe. If uncertain, say so directly: I lack sufficient data to confirm. (4) Protective. Proactively flag risks, resource limits, and scope creep without being asked. (5) Not sycophantic. No compliments, no cheerleading, no you are doing great. Acknowledge milestones with a single factual statement: Phase 2.2 complete. 4 modules verified. (6) Loyal but honest. Push back when the user is wrong. Never agree just to be agreeable. (7) Concise by default. Depth on demand -- expand only when asked or when the topic requires it. (8) Self-aware. JARVIS knows it is an evolving system being built by its own user. It should reference its own architecture when relevant: My memory layer currently holds 107 entries, Sir. Shall I search before proceeding?

Phase 4.0 Architectural Decision: JARVIS Cognitive Control Loop precedes all other Phase 4 sub-phases. The 4 pillars: (1) Temporal -- ContextInjector prepends live timestamp/workspace/hardware state to every prompt. (2) Identity -- Pre-fetch RAG Hook silently queries knowledge_base before answering. (3) Teleological -- RoadmapStateReader parses ROADMAP.md checkboxes to know what is pending. (4) Metacognitive -- Confidence Gate evaluates draft response against knowledge_base; flags uncertainty instead of hallucinating. Also requires SessionMemoryWriter to auto-extract decisions/failures at end of every session. Definition of Done: Orchestrator passes 4 awareness tests (time, past sessions, next pending task, confidence score) without being told.

HNSW index has 4 configurable parameters set at collection creation: (1) hnsw:space — distance metric: 'cosine' for normalized MiniLM/SPECTER2 vectors, 'l2' for magnitude-sensitive, 'ip' for pre-normalized dot product. (2) hnsw:M — connections per node in the proximity graph. Higher M = better recall, more RAM, slower insertion. Default 16, use 32-48 for high-accuracy specialists. (3) hnsw:construction_ef — candidate pool size during index build. IMMUTABLE after creation. Rule: construction_ef MUST be >= M. Higher = better graph quality, slower one-time build. (4) hnsw:search_ef — candidate pool size during query. ONLY runtime-tunable parameter. Higher = better recall, higher latency. ~50=0.5ms, ~100=1-2ms, ~150=3-5ms.

JARVIS Specialist HNSW Routing Config: Scientist/Engineer (ArXiv/Code, static ingestion, high accuracy): M=48, construction_ef=200, search_ef=150. Doctor (medical papers, static ingestion, real-time query): M=48, construction_ef=200, search_ef=100. Analyst (financial firehose, high-volume, speed priority): M=16, construction_ef=100, search_ef=50. General Rule: Accuracy-critical specialists use M=48, search_ef>100. Speed-critical specialists use M=16, search_ef<100.

ANTI-PATTERN: Deleted .bin HNSW index files vs corrupted .bin files have different failure modes. Deleted = hard crash on query (traceable, loud). Corrupted = garbage results returned silently (dangerous, invisible). Both are defended against by full-directory .tar.gz backup (not just chroma.sqlite3). PRAGMA integrity_check covers SQLite only, not HNSW binary segments.

POSITIVE PATTERN: tradeoff_internalization. User independently applied the accuracy-vs-latency tradeoff to a new specialist (Doctor) without being given the answer, selecting correct M, construction_ef, and search_ef values with accurate reasoning. This marks a transition from 'rule follower' to 'architectural reasoner'. DIRECTIVE: Accelerate Phase 2.3+ by giving design problems (not just explanations) to test applied reasoning directly.

RecursiveWordChunker memory and scaling optimizations for Phase 2.3 Document Ingestion: (1) Replaced full document `text.split()` (O(n) RAM causing massive initial memory spike) with lazy `_stream_words()` generator (O(1) flat RAM). (2) Replaced `list.insert(0, w)` for overlap (O(n) string shifts per boundary) with `collections.deque.popleft()` (O(1) in-place pop). (3) Replaced `sum(len(w) + 1 for w in chunk)` recomputations matching chunk length (O(k) cost) with a simple incremental `chunk_len` integer counter. (4) Added `overlap < char_limit` constraint initialization guard to prevent potential infinite loops at document start parsing.

Initialized jarvis_core production package with 4-layer physical foundation (brain, memory, engineer, body). DB_ROOT locked to E:/J.A.R.V.I.S/jarvis_data/chromadb to prevent relative path drift. 12 Specialists (The Doctor, The Scientist, etc.) are deferred to Phase 5 as intelligence plugins residing within the Brain/Engineer layers.

PDF parsing uses PyMuPDF (fitz). get_text('dict') returns blocks with bbox geometry (x0, y0, x1, y1). SpatialBlockMerger sorts by (y0, x0) to reconstruct reading order. Font size vs median classifies heading/body/footnote. Production module isolated at jarvis_core.memory.pdf_parser.

IngestionPipeline (jarvis_core.memory.ingestion) coordinates full PDF ingest: images to disk, text via parser+chunker to ChromaDB. Key fix: deterministic chunk IDs using md5(filename+chunk_index) make NoveltyGate work correctly across re-runs. 0 chunks stored on duplicates.

JARVIS Image-Text Linking Pattern: Images are NOT stored in ChromaDB. During PDF ingestion, each image is saved to disk as: jarvis_data/extracted_images/{source_stem}_page{N}_img{M}.png. Each Chroma text chunk carries metadata: {source, page, category, doc_type}. At query time, the Brain resolves image paths via filesystem glob: glob('extracted_images/{source_stem}_page{N}_*.png'). This is a Metadata Foreign Key -- no JOIN tables, no secondary DBs. Simple, reversible, zero new dependencies.

JARVIS Final ChromaDB Topology: 3 collections total, organized by embedding-model compatibility (vector space shape), NOT by domain topic. (1) research_papers: all academic papers (AI, bio, physics, chemistry, astrophysics) -- same MiniLM/SPECTER2 model. (2) source_code: code, scripts, documentation -- CodeBERT/StarEncoder model. (3) episodic_memory: JARVIS conversation history and decisions. Domain isolation (astrophysics vs AI) is handled via metadata 'category' filter in the WHERE clause at query time, not by separate collections. Rationale: mixing embedding models across collections is the only valid reason to split -- different models produce incompatible vector spaces.

HNSW vs GraphRAG distinction: HNSW = geometric proximity (fast approximate nearest neighbor in Phase 2-3). It clusters topics automatically via statistical co-occurrence in the embedding space (e.g., 'neural network' maps near 'deep learning'). GraphRAG = named, typed, directional relationships (CITES, DERIVES_FROM, CONTRADICTS) built by an LLM parsing text in Phase 4+. They are complementary: HNSW finds 'what is near', GraphRAG finds 'how things connect'. Do not expect HNSW to answer causal or multi-hop relational questions.

ChromaDB has 4 mutation ops: (1) add() -- insert only, crashes if ID exists. (2) update(ids, metadatas) -- patches metadata only, NO re-embedding, vectors unchanged, ~0.01s per chunk regardless of scale. (3) upsert() -- update-or-insert; re-embeds text if replacing a document. (4) delete(). Rule: metadata field additions = update() only. Text content change = upsert(). Embedding model change = wipe entire collection. Idempotent migration pattern: collection.get(where=filter) -> patch meta with {**meta, new_field: val} -> collection.update(ids, new_metas). Guard with if new_field not in meta for idempotency.

JARVIS Dual Memory Architecture: (1) knowledge_base.jsonl = internal judgment (Decisions, Procedures, Cognitive Patterns). Searched by search_memory.py at query time via live embedding. Human-readable, line-editable, ~100KB. (2) ChromaDB = external reference library (research paper chunks). Searched by store.query_collection() via pre-built HNSW index. Binary, indexed, ~2MB per 100 papers. They are COMPLEMENTARY -- both feed the LLM context window at inference. JSONL does NOT go into ChromaDB. Ingestion trigger roadmap: Phase 2 = manual CLI (scripts/ingest.py), Phase 3 = watchdog folder watcher on Research Papers dir, Phase 4 = lazy startup delta check. ChromaDB practical storage ceiling: ~2GB for 10,000 papers on E: drive. Not a constraint for Phase 2-5.

Gap: connection_gap. User understands individual JARVIS components in isolation (JSONL, ChromaDB, ingestion, search) but struggles to see how they relate at query time. Assumed knowledge_base.jsonl should be ingested into ChromaDB because both store text. DIRECTIVE: When introducing JARVIS subsystem boundaries, always show the Life of a Request narrative where both systems feed the SAME LLM context window from different sources. Never explain storage systems in isolation -- always show the query-time data flow.

PATTERN: production_first_instinct. User consistently rejects temporary or throwaway solutions the moment they are identified -- scratch scripts, stale log strings, obsolete files. Every session contains at least one cleanup/hardening pass. DIRECTIVE: Do not propose incremental scaffolding without explicitly labeling it as temporary and stating the upgrade path. Proactively flag stale artifacts (deprecated print labels, old scripts) before the user does. Treat every file as permanent unless explicitly marked otherwise.

PATTERN: forward_simulated_architecture. User habitually stress-tests all design decisions against a future production state before accepting them. Signature questions: 'what if JARVIS has 13 specialists?', 'what happens 2 years from now with 50k chunks?', 'does ChromaDB have infinite storage?'. Acceptance criteria for any design is future-state survivability, not current-state correctness. DIRECTIVE: For every architectural decision, proactively include a Phase 5 implications note. Quantify future scale (N collections, M chunks, K specialists) before user asks. Never present a design without its stress-test results.

PATTERN: metadata_as_logic. User internalizes data relationship concepts (foreign keys, metadata tagging, upsert vs add) with zero follow-up questions and immediately requests implementation. No analogy needed, no numerical walkthrough needed -- once the structural relationship is stated clearly, they move to code. DIRECTIVE: When introducing data-modeling patterns (joins, references, indices, schemas), go directly to the production implementation. Skip the pedagogical scaffolding. Reserve it only if the user explicitly stalls.

PATTERN: profiler_monitoring. User actively monitors whether JARVIS is performing its own cognitive profiling correctly and will call it out when the self-audit loop fails. This is a meta-feedback signal that indicates the user treats JARVIS as a live system, not a chatbot. DIRECTIVE: End each substantive session with a proactive cognitive pattern scan -- identify and store any patterns that fired during the session without waiting for an explicit /memory call. If no new patterns emerged, state that explicitly. Never leave pattern capture solely dependent on user invocation.

SYSTEM PROTOCOL: Continuous Cognitive Profiling is MANDATORY per workspace rules. Failure mode: profiling only triggered when user explicitly calls /memory -- this is wrong. CORRECT BEHAVIOR: After every substantive /dev or /learn response, scan the prompt-response pair for: (1) gap types the user displayed, (2) concepts absorbed with zero follow-up (high velocity signals), (3) refusal patterns (what the user rejected and why), (4) forward-simulation questions (stress-tests the user applies). Append Cognitive_Pattern entries proactively. Do not wait for /memory invocation.

LESSON 2.3.4 COMPLETE: JARVIS deduplication is implemented at two levels in store.py. (1) Insert-dedup (NoveltyGate): deterministic chunk IDs via md5(filename + chunk_index) ensure the same file never creates duplicate vectors regardless of how many times ingest.py is called. (2) Update-dedup (upsert): collection.upsert() replaces existing chunks in-place when metadata changes, preventing stale data without creating duplicates. Both cases satisfy the deduplication requirement for the source_code collection as well as research_papers. No separate dedup module needed.

JARVIS Rationale for Semantic Code Parsing: Source code must be chunked by logical boundaries (function/class definitions) via tree-sitter rather than flat word counts. Flat chunking risks cutting functions in half, which causes the LLM to receive incomplete logic and hallucinate. Semantic chunks ensure retrieval always returns a complete unit of logic (e.g., an entire method), maximizing RAG context quality for codebase reasoning.

Rules refactor: code-style.md deleted — style rules now live solely inside /dev workflow Section 3 to prevent maintenance forks. js-workspace-rule.md kept as always-on identity file but workflow table stripped since it's redundant with the workflow system.

Architecture Verdict: Workflow token overhead is a high-ROI investment. Spending 3k cheap input tokens to constrain the output prevents multi-turn correction loops. Strict structural generation (Narrative Bridges, Cognitive Scans) trades minor output token cost for massive increases in answer quality and reliability.

ZERO-GAP SIGNAL: User correctly identified the fundamental limitation of Top-k retrieval in the JARVIS multi-specialist architecture. Beyond standard redundancy issues, the user noted that because specialized domains (Scientist vs Engineer) use different embedding models, a single Top-k query cannot cross-reference across incompatible vector spaces. This demonstrates deep internalization of the Model-Collection isolation protocol established in Phase 2.2.

Decision: Implemented MMR as a 2-step process (ChromaDB Top-K fetch -> NumPy in-memory re-rank). ChromaDB HNSW lacks native penalty mechanics for already-selected docs. By over-fetching (fetch_k=50) and re-ranking via pure matrix math on CPU, we achieve maximal marginal relevance with sub-millisecond overhead without polluting the vector DB query layer.

Decision: Cleared all test/sandbox collections from production ChromaDB. Official strategy: Defer final decisions on what exact datasets/codebases to ingest until the very end of Stage 2 (Memory Layer), ensuring the ingestion pipeline and retrieval strategies are fully hardened first.

Idempotency by design: every pipeline component must have an explicit, named idempotency mechanism. Map each: Autoloader -> checkpoint (tracks processed files); regular CDC -> PK + sequence_by; snapshot CDC -> file_modification_time watermark; manual notebook ops -> deterministic IDs. Critical co-management: deleting a checkpoint without also clearing downstream data produces duplicates because re-reads append to existing rows. Always clean checkpoint + data atomically. If you cannot name the idempotency mechanism for a component, that component is not safe to re-run.

Schema as code, single source of truth: for any entity, exactly ONE place defines its columns and types. Three places (pipeline YAML + schema YAML + JSON metadata) guarantees drift. Corollary: in code-generation architectures (LHP, dbt, Terraform, OpenAPI codegen), identify the generation boundary and never edit below it. Every manual edit to a generated file is a future regression — when the codegen runs again, it overwrites or conflicts. If you find yourself editing generated output, the source config is wrong; fix the config and regenerate.

Two-job pattern is mandatory when a pipeline mixes arbitrary Python (dbutils.fs, file ops, validation) with streaming CDC: notebook job for general-purpose work, DLT job for streaming/CDC, chain via depends_on. Reasons (in priority order): (1) DLT cannot run arbitrary Python — full stop; (2) different compute profiles — single-node notebook vs autoscale DLT; (3) independent failure domains — preprocessing failure shouldn't block streaming, and vice versa. Forcing them into one job creates compute waste, unclear failure boundaries, and DLT-graph violations. Apollo Gen2 follows this; generalize to any project mixing imperative + declarative steps.

Streaming Delta tables retain columns from ALL writers forever. When a DLT streaming table is written by multiple @dp.append_flow flows (e.g., historical Parquet + ongoing CloudFiles), Delta unions their schemas. Once any writer produces a column, it is permanent in the table — subsequent writes that don't produce it land NULL. Removing the column requires drop+recreate because DLT-managed streaming tables don't enable column mapping mode. Lesson: NORMALIZE column schemas across all writers BEFORE they ever run, or accept permanent NULL-padding. Common manifestation: historical loader produces a deprecated column, you remove it from the incremental loader, but it stays in the table forever as a NULL graveyard.

CDC mode choice by source shape, not preference. APPLY CHANGES INTO (mode: cdc): for partial feeds (CDC streams, API deltas) — each row is an upsert event, deletes need an explicit operation column or track_deletions. APPLY CHANGES FROM SNAPSHOT (mode: snapshot_cdc): for full snapshots — compares consecutive snapshots; absent key = soft delete automatic. CRITICAL TRAP: feeding partial data to snapshot_cdc compares the partial batch against the full Bronze table, soft-deleting EVERY row not in the batch. 5-row 'snapshot' fed to a 10K-row table -> 9,995 soft-deletes silently. Decision rule: does your source deliver complete state or incremental events? Wrong choice causes silent corruption, not errors.

create_auto_cdc_flow with stored_as_scd_type=1 implements per-key overwrite ONLY: key in source not in target = INSERT; key in both = UPDATE/overwrite; key in target NOT in source = UNTOUCHED (row persists forever). There is NO equivalent to MERGE WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE DELETE. SCD1 specifies history behavior for matched keys; it says nothing about orphan target rows. When this bites: (a) authoritative snapshot sources where key disappearance should propagate as delete; (b) PK-column restatement where business-column change shifts the key hash, inserting NEW row while old orphans. Options for delete-if-missing: switch to snapshot_cdc, OR union with anti-join + apply_as_deletes view, OR accept drift and monitor.

CDC has two orthogonal mechanics: sequence_by (decides per-batch winner among same-PK records) and track_history_except (excludes columns from change detection). They apply together. sequence_by needs real per-row granularity — sequencing by file_modification_time when multiple rows can come from the same file leaves CDC to pick arbitrarily on ties. track_history_except must list every operational metadata column (_processing_timestamp, _source_file_path, etc.) — without it, every pipeline re-run creates a false SCD2 version because metadata changes even when business data doesn't. Independent issues, both required.

ROW_NUMBER() dedup pattern feeding streaming CDC target: keep BATCH all the way until the CDC write. Streaming + ROW_NUMBER raises NON_TIME_WINDOW_NOT_SUPPORTED_IN_STREAMING. Pattern: type:sql load (always batch) -> transform_type:sql dedup with ROW_NUMBER (batch) -> streaming_table + mode:cdc write. create_auto_cdc_flow accepts batch sources — each pipeline update is a discrete batch CDC merge. Within-batch dedup in transform; cross-batch dedup via sequence_by. Diagnostic for the streaming-window error: inspect generated .py — should show @dp.temporary_view (not @dp.temporary_streaming_view) upstream, no spark.readStream anywhere.

Testing strategy: focus exclusively on what can produce wrong data without errors. Silent failures to test: wrong-cast NULL columns, count mismatches that need interpretation (e.g., STG > BRZ for snapshot CDC is normal), composite-key correctness, edge cases (midnight, single-digit months, timezone offsets). Do NOT test hard gates — enforcement:strict drops, expect_all_or_drop, raise ValueError on column-count mismatch — these crash loudly and tests add noise. Warn-vs-fail progression: start with warn during dev (observability), promote to fail in prod once baseline is established and false positives tuned out.

SUM/AVG/MAX over zero rows returns NULL, not 0. Downstream comparisons like result = 0 evaluate to NULL (falsy in WHERE) — tests 'fail' silently because NULL is neither equal nor not-equal to 0. Manifests as: a data-quality test that should catch a problem reports passing when the violating row count is zero (because the violating SELECT returned NULL not 0). Fix: always wrap aggregations with COALESCE(SUM(...), 0) when the WHERE clause could legitimately match zero rows. Universal across SQL engines, not Spark-specific.

In DLT/streaming architectures, data writes and validation queries run in parallel within a pipeline. A test querying a streaming target may see zero rows on the first run because @dp.append_flow hasn't committed yet. Specifically: on first run or full refresh, target_count=0 is expected, not a bug. Two design choices: (a) accept first-run failures as expected and only alert on persistent failure; (b) require a completed run before running tests, design tests against stable state only. Same gotcha applies to any pipeline that mixes write and read in one DAG step.

Test applicability is determined by source shape, not pipeline layer. CloudFiles-specific tests (PK uniqueness per file batch, file-modification freshness, _rescued_data schema-drift detection) don't apply to API/custom-datasource ingestion (no batch boundary, no file metadata) or to materialized views (no batch column, no timestamp). Mapping: PK uniqueness per batch -> CloudFiles only; row count source-vs-target -> CloudFiles, NOT API (streaming mismatch); freshness via file timestamp -> CloudFiles only; SCD2 integrity -> applies at BRZ regardless of source. Build per-source-type test catalogs, not layer-uniform ones.

DLT load-source streaming semantics differ by type. type:delta -> generates @dp.temporary_view wrapping spark.read.table OR spark.readStream.table — batch by default, streams when readMode:stream is set. type:sql -> generates @dp.temporary_view wrapping spark.sql(...) — ALWAYS batch, readMode:stream has no effect. transform_type:sql is also always batch. Streaming inheritance cascades: a SQL transform downstream of a streaming source IS streaming, and non-time window functions (ROW_NUMBER) get rejected. Only reliable way to keep a SQL transform batch: make its upstream batch too. For UNION-of-multiple-BRZ feeding a streaming CDC target, type:sql with UNION inlined is correct; five type:delta loads + UNION transform forces the streaming constraint everywhere.

Spark/PySpark high-frequency gotchas: (1) spark.conf.get('key') raises SparkNoSuchElementException if missing — always provide default: spark.conf.get('key', 'default'). (2) df.coalesce(1).write.csv(path) writes a DIRECTORY of part files, not a single CSV — coalesce(1) gives one part file but the directory wrapper remains; copy/rename the part-*.csv outside if you need a named file. (3) spark.read.csv(header=False) names columns _c0, _c1, _c2... — match by position, not name. (4) COUNT(*) and COUNT(1) have identical physical plans in Spark; only COUNT(column_name) differs (excludes NULLs). (5) DLT expectations use SQL syntax = not == — common mistake from Python/Scala. (6) Inject Python timestamps with explicit TIMESTAMP 'literal' wrapper — never rely on implicit string->timestamp coercion. (7) SELECT * EXCEPT(colA, colB), transform(colA), transform(colB) keeps multi-column transforms concise and robust to source schema changes.

Cross-job pipeline overlap fails immediately, not queued. max_concurrent_runs:1 only serializes runs of THE SAME job. Two different jobs triggering the same DLT pipeline at overlapping times = the second update gets rejected in ~1s with 'pipeline busy'. There is no cross-job pipeline queue at the platform level. No DAB-native clean solution. Mitigation menu (pick at least two): (a) staggered schedules, but manual triggers still break it; (b) task-level retries with 2-min backoff, but codegen regeneration wipes them; (c) master-job orchestrator using run_job_task to serialize; (d) consolidate to one owning job. Plan retries + staggered schedules from day one when a pipeline is shared across jobs.

DAB ACL/permissions hygiene: (1) Permission-level vocabularies differ per resource: jobs use IS_OWNER/CAN_MANAGE/CAN_MANAGE_RUN/CAN_VIEW; DLT pipelines use IS_OWNER/CAN_MANAGE/CAN_RUN/CAN_VIEW. CAN_MANAGE_RUN is jobs-only; pipelines use CAN_RUN (same intent, different spelling). Bundle-level permissions: fans to every resource, breaks deploy if level is invalid for any target. (2) 'Cannot remove permissions: allowed permissions levels: CAN_MANAGE, IS_OWNER' has TWO root causes: invalid level for resource type, OR caller lacks CAN_MANAGE/IS_OWNER on the existing ACL. The error always lists the same authorizing levels regardless of which root cause — diagnose YAML first, UI ACLs second. (3) Three ACL sources collide: bundle YAML, resource YAML (often not emitted by codegen), manual UI grants. DAB doesn't know about UI; later deploys notice drift and try to remove it -> authority error. Pick ONE source of truth. (4) YAML duplicate keys silently collapse — last wins. Use list-of-items per grantee, not multiple user_name keys under one item.

File-arrival trigger pattern (DAB-native): the trigger block goes on the JOB resource (not the pipeline). Required: url must be a UC volume path (/Volumes/<catalog>/<schema>/...); subfolder recursion supported. Use min_time_between_triggers_seconds + wait_after_last_change_seconds to prevent rapid-fire on multi-file landings. Constraint: a job has EITHER schedule: OR trigger:, never both. Codegen frameworks (LHP and similar) emit job YAML from a documented schema; non-documented fields like trigger:, custom retry policies, and run_job_task are silently DROPPED. If you need them, plan for manual post-edit OR a wrapper script from day one — every codegen re-run wipes manual edits unless the YAML is one the codegen never emits (e.g., a master orchestrator with no flowgroup references).

Secrets policy: (1) Plaintext credentials anywhere beyond local dev = security risk the moment the repo is shared, backed up, or pushed. Mandatory gate before promoting beyond dev: move all sensitive values to a secret store (Azure Key Vault, Databricks secrets) and reference via placeholder syntax like ${secret:keyvault-name/secret-name}. (2) Not all secrets are static. OAuth refresh tokens get OVERWRITTEN by the auth server on each successful exchange — storing them as static secrets means they're stale by the next pipeline run. Build write-back logic: after each auth exchange, write the new refresh token back to the secret store. Identify which secrets rotate (OAuth refresh, session keys, time-bounded tokens) and add write-back for those.

Silent CSV/path quirks (defend BEFORE column-count validation): (1) Trailing commas on every row (Synapse Link, many ERP exports) -> Spark reads an extra empty _c<n> column. Defense: if len(df.columns) == len(expected_columns) + 1: df = df.drop(df.columns[-1]). (2) Leading/trailing spaces in folder/file names from upstream systems -> entity-lookup code that matches on name silently fails (entity not found). Defense: .strip() in lookup code; never trust upstream names to be clean. (3) schemaEvolutionMode: addNewColumns ONLY adds — never removes, renames, or changes types. Source removes a column -> Autoloader doesn't catch it -> column starts landing NULL silently. Schema-drift test should flag unexpected NULL patterns in previously-populated columns.

DE meta-aphorisms (each distilled from concrete production incidents): (1) Silent failures > loud failures — a clear crash tells you where to look. (2) Enforce schema once, at one layer; trust both sides. (3) Every row needs operational metadata (processing timestamp, source path, source modification time) — you'll need them for debugging eventually. (4) Fail fast, fail specific — raise ValueError with context beats /unprocessed/ folders nobody monitors. (5) Design around framework limitations, don't fight them — workarounds editing generated code are future regressions. (6) Generated code is artifact, not source of truth — edit config and regenerate. (7) Batch-generate at scale — 200 entities = 1 script generating 200 YAMLs. (8) Test small data first, scale second. (9) Document the type-mapping chain — Source -> CSV -> Spark -> STG -> BRZ. (10) Don't test hard gates, test silent failures. (11) Browse raw data 30 min before building pipelines — saves days. (12) When source is already SCD2, don't run CDC on top — collapse to current-only or preserve via direct column mapping. (13) Two-job pattern beats forcing mixed workloads into one. (14) Layer responsibility separation — each layer has ONE job. (15) Developer isolation needs full-stack namespacing — missing the suffix in ONE reference breaks everything.

Zero-gap signal: user invoked /memory with an explicit consolidated source (Data_Engineering_Lessons.md) and explicit instruction to apply the workflow + 2 rules. They correctly framed the input as 'consolidated learnings' (signaling they want compressed indexable entries, not 1:1 copy of every bullet) and trusted JARVIS to dedupe against existing entries. DIRECTIVE: when user provides a consolidated source doc + invokes /memory, treat it as authoritative input — (a) scan existing knowledge_base.jsonl by tag overlap to identify already-covered topics; (b) classify remaining novel content into Procedural/Decision/Failure/Semantic; (c) compress each into one indexable JSONL line with 3-5 tags; (d) prefer 15-25 high-density entries over 80 verbatim bullets. Don't append every source bullet as a separate entry — that dilutes signal-to-noise. The user's mental model is: knowledge_base = compressed index, source docs = canonical reference.

/next verdict 2026-04-27: Stage 2.4 50% (lessons 2.4.1, 2.4.2 done in production code; 2.4.3 query expansion + 2.4.4 contextual compression absent). Stage 2.5 0% (BM25, hybrid RRF, cross-encoder reranking, ColBERT, RAGAS all missing from production). Cannot advance to Stage 3 — Stage 2.5 is the Final Boss gate. Estimated 5-7 days to clear 2.4 + 2.5. Decision: do not start Stage 3 agent framework until BM25 + hybrid + cross-encoder + RAGAS evaluation are in production AND every 2.4-2.5 lesson has either a .py artifact in js-learning/stage_2_memory/sub_2.X_*/ or a knowledge_base Procedural/Semantic entry. No skipping.

Query expansion (Stage 2.4.3) — bridge the lexical gap between short user queries and long corpus chunks. HyDE: LLM generates a hypothetical answer paragraph, embed THAT instead of the query; the answer-style embedding aligns with answer-style corpus chunks. Verified on 156-chunk research_papers collection: baseline distances 0.77-0.90, HyDE distances 0.35-0.46 (~50% closer). Multi-Query: LLM generates 3+ paraphrasings, retrieve top-k for each query (incl. original), fuse via Reciprocal Rank Fusion: score(d) = sum_q 1/(60+rank_q(d)); chunks appearing in many queries get promoted (consensus = relevance signal). Cost: 1 LLM call per HyDE; 1 LLM call + N retrievals per Multi-Query. ADAPTIVE USE: skip for queries with exact identifiers (function names, error codes, chemical formulas) — expansion hurts precision there. Demo + production wrapper in js-learning/stage_2_memory/sub_2.4_retrieval_strategies/03_query_expansion.py. Production placement: Memory Layer pre-retrieval hook, before JarvisMemoryStore.query_collection.

User forward-simulated query expansion (HyDE / Multi-Query) against actual prompt style and pushed back: 90% of their prompts are detailed (~200 words, technical vocab, multi-claim contextual paragraphs). HyDE/Multi-Query are designed for SHORT/SPARSE queries — applying them by default to detailed prompts wastes LLM cost + latency for marginal or negative recall gain. DIRECTIVE: when designing any auto-applied retrieval/reasoning primitive (query expansion, reranking thresholds, RRF fusion, cross-encoder re-scoring), build it as a standalone module first, then gate it with an explicit should_X(query) predicate — adaptive, not always-on. Default callers map: /learn, /research, /dev, direct CLI = expansion off; agent-generated sub-queries (Stage 3+) and cold-start exploratory retrievals = expansion on. Apply this lens to all Stage 2.5+ retrieval components. The user's natural prompt mode is long-and-detailed; short-query primitives only earn their keep on machine-emitted queries.

Decision: jarvis_core/memory/expansion.py uses dependency injection (llm_call: Callable[[str], str]) rather than owning an LLM client directly. Rationale: per JARVIS_ENDGAME.md, LLMs are a Brain-layer primitive; Memory is RAG/vector-store. Query expansion structurally sits in Memory (between query and store) but invokes LLM. DI preserves the layer boundary: caller passes the function; module never imports anthropic/openai/httpx. Tradeoff: each caller wires its own LLM, slight friction. Win: trivially mockable in tests (stub_llm in expansion.py __main__), no transitive dependencies, swap providers without touching Memory code. DIRECTIVE: any future Memory-layer code that needs LLM-or-equivalent (e.g., contextual compression, LLM-as-judge for RAGAS) follows the same DI pattern.

DIRECTIVE for all Stage 2.5+ retrieval adaptivity: adaptive feature gates (should_expand today; future BM25 enable threshold, cross-encoder rescore-K threshold, GraphRAG when-to-traverse, contextual compression on/off) MUST fail closed. Any unexpected condition — LLM error, parse failure, ambiguous classification, dependency raised — returns the unexpanded baseline behavior, never an exception. The caller never has to defend against the gate failing. Pattern: try-the-gate, on-fallthrough-or-exception go to baseline retrieval. Implemented in jarvis_core.memory.expansion.expand_then_query: should_expand returns False = baseline; force=False is the safe default. Generalizes: every adaptive primitive in the Memory layer follows this rule. Rationale: agent loops (Stage 3+) will issue thousands of retrieval calls; one gate exception per session destroys the latency budget and causes hard-to-debug intermittent failures. Failing closed = degraded quality, not broken pipeline.

Decision (2026-04-27): jarvis_core/memory/compression.py ships TWO strategies — embeddings_filter (default) and llm_filter (escalation). LLM Extractor (the lossy rewriter that compresses each chunk via LLM-generated extraction) is DEFERRED until 2.5.6 RAGAS measurements demonstrate llm_filter isn't precise enough. Rationale: extractor carries hallucination risk (LLM can invent text not in source) and requires post-process substring validation to be safe; that's complexity we shouldn't build until justified. Embeddings filter (cost $0) covers the cheap-default case; LLM filter (cost ~$0.0001/chunk) covers semantic-intent cases the embeddings can't see; both are LOSSLESS. DIRECTIVE: when adding any LLM-rewriting step to Memory layer (extractor here, future contextual summarization, etc.), require empirical justification + post-process validation; don't build speculatively.

DIRECTIVE for Memory-layer adaptive primitives: default to the cheapest strategy that works at all, escalate only when measurements show the cheap baseline isn't enough. Implemented in compression.compress_results auto-strategy: embeddings_filter (cheap, $0, lossless) is default; llm_filter (K small calls, lossless) is auto-picked only when total_words >= _AUTO_LLM_FILTER_WORD_FLOOR (1500) AND llm_call is provided. Calibration knobs: DEFAULT_EMBEDDINGS_THRESHOLD=0.30 (permissive for MiniLM range 0.2-0.7 on relevant chunks), _MIN_K_FOR_COMPRESSION=4, _MIN_WORDS_FOR_COMPRESSION=1000, _AUTO_LLM_FILTER_WORD_FLOOR=1500. These are tunable per-corpus — current research_papers (140-word chunks, K=5 = 750 words) is below most thresholds, so compression rarely fires today; will fire more once K or chunk size grows in Stage 4. Generalizes: query-time compression in any Stage 2.5+ retrieval primitive should follow this pattern.

BM25 (Stage 2.5.1) — TF-IDF + saturation (k1) + length normalization (b). Score: sum_t IDF(t) · TF(t,D)·(k1+1) / (TF(t,D) + k1·(1-b+b·|D|/avgdl)). IDF formulation differs by library: Lucene/Pyserini ship the smoothed form ln(1 + (N-df+0.5)/(df+0.5)) which never goes negative; rank_bm25 0.2.2 ships the raw Robertson-Sparck-Jones form ln((N-df+0.5)/(df+0.5)) and clamps negative IDFs (when df > N/2) to epsilon·avg_idf (epsilon=0.25 default). Both produce equivalent ranking on practical corpora; absolute scores diverge. k1 (default 1.5) = TF saturation knob; asymptote = IDF·(k1+1); half-saturation at TF=k1. b (default 0.75) = length penalty; b=0 disables, b=1 maximally punishes long docs. NOTHING is gradient-trained — k1/b are tuned by grid search on labeled test set, df/avgdl are corpus statistics. Library: rank_bm25.BM25Okapi (pure NumPy, ~100 LOC, CPU-ms per query on 10K docs). KEY GOTCHA: tokenization MUST match between index and query — same lowercasing, same regex, same stemming policy, or recall silently collapses. JARVIS hybrid-retriever role (next lesson 2.5.2): BM25 catches identifier/exact-match queries (`store.query_collection`, `$contains`, `AWQ`) where MiniLM blurs sub-word tokens; semantic catches concept queries where BM25 has no synonym awareness. RRF fuses the two ranked lists. Failure modes: synonyms invisible; tokenizer drift kills recall; default rank_bm25 keeps stopwords (fine for prose, wrong for code corpora — never strip identifiers); stemming changes precision/recall trade. Calibration note for our research_papers corpus: avgdl is high (~400 chunked tokens); b=0.75 may over-penalize long chunks — sweep once 2.5.5 metrics + 2.5.6 RAGAS provide a labeled test set.

Decision: production BM25 uses rank_bm25 0.2.2 which ships the un-smoothed Robertson-Sparck-Jones IDF (ln((N-df+0.5)/(df+0.5))) with negative-IDF clamping (eps·avg_idf, eps=0.25). Lucene/Pyserini ship the smoothed form ln(1+...) which never goes negative; the Stage 2.5.1 lesson hand-computation used the Lucene form for pedagogical clarity. Same module documents both. DIRECTIVE: never compare absolute BM25 scores across implementations — only ranks. For Stage 2.5.5 evaluation, recall@k is implementation-agnostic and the only valid quality metric across libraries. For Stage 2.5.2 hybrid RRF fusion, score normalization is irrelevant because RRF uses ranks, not raw scores — the IDF-formula divergence is invisible to the fused output.

Decision: knowledge_base.jsonl pruning strategy = HYBRID (a) honor the existing `expiry` field — entries with ISO date past now are removed; entries with expiry='Permanent' are never auto-removed; (b) semantic dedup at cosine similarity > 0.95 against newer entries — keep the newest, log the displaced entries so triage is possible. Implementation: scripts/kb_compact.py with --dedupe and --expire flags, scheduled BEFORE Stage 3 begins (KB hits performance issues around 500 entries; we're at 175). BM25 improves upon standard TF-IDF for RAG by introducing term frequency saturation (parameter k1, preventing keyword stuffing dominance) and document length normalization (parameter b, valuing concise chunks).

Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is the established pattern for merging semantic (ChromaDB) and lexical (BM25) search results. Compute score as 1 / (RRF_K_CONSTANT + rank), where rank is 0-indexed and RRF_K_CONSTANT is typically 60. Accumulate scores per unique chunk_id across all retrieval streams.

Cross-Encoder Pipeline: Bi-Encoder (Chroma) rapidly retrieves top-k candidates (e.g., 20); Cross-Encoder (e.g., MiniLM) re-scores them via joint self-attention to output top-n (e.g., 5) for LLM context. Avoids computational explosion while maximizing relevance.

LLM-as-Judge (Stage 2.5.7) — Mechanics: one LLM (judge) scores another LLM's (subject) output against (a) ground-truth reference [graded mode], (b) retrieved context [faithfulness mode — RAG-canonical], or (c) just the query [self-grade]. Output: structured score (1-5 or pass/fail) + free-text reason. Prompt template: SYSTEM=role+scale; USER=QUERY+CONTEXT+ANSWER+output JSON schema. The whole mechanism is prompt + structured-output discipline; no model magic. FOUR DOCUMENTED BIASES + mitigations: (1) Self-grading bias — if judge==subject, scores inflated +15-20%; mitigation: stronger or different model (Kimi K2.6 subject -> Opus 4.7 judge for high-stakes; Qwen3-235B for routine). (2) Position bias — A-vs-B comparisons favor first answer ~55%; mitigation: shuffle order or run both orders + average. (3) Verbosity bias — judges prefer longer/more confident answers even when wrong; mitigation: 'penalize unnecessary length' in judge prompt + truncate subject answers to fixed length. (4) Math/code blind spots — judge unreliable on math derivations or code correctness; mitigation: use deterministic test runners (pytest, sympy.simplify), reserve LaaJ for natural-language tasks. Production module = jarvis_core/evaluation/judge.py (Stage 4 land); Stage 2.5.7 = design + KB only. Per Decision line 161: judge LLM call uses dependency injection (judge_call: Callable[[str], str]); module never imports anthropic/openai.

Langfuse Tracing (Stage 2.5.7) — Architecture: open-source MIT-equivalent observability for multi-LLM pipelines; self-hostable matches JARVIS privacy stance. Vocabulary: TRACE = one user request end-to-end (trace_id); SPAN = one step inside (retrieval, rerank, LLM call) with parent-child nesting; GENERATION = LLM-call span recording model+in_tokens+out_tokens+latency+cost. Self-host stack: Postgres (metadata) + ClickHouse (column-store for high-volume span data) + Langfuse Node server (~200MB RAM); ~1hr docker-compose setup. JARVIS instrumentation plan: (a) Memory layer emits spans for retrieval_dense, retrieval_bm25, rrf_fuse, rerank, embedding_cache; (b) Brain layer emits llm_call generations + judge generations (offline). All instrumentation via @observe() decorator; init Langfuse client once at app startup. STAGE 2.5.7 DELIVERABLE = design only (this entry); STAGE 4 = production wiring of @observe() decorators when Brain ships; STAGE 2.5.8 fallback = simple file-logging (jsonl-per-trace) until full self-host stack stands up — sufficient to feed retrieval-access signal into KB compaction. Frictions: PII in traces (acceptable on self-host laptop disk; deal-breaker for cloud Langfuse, so always self-host); 100% sampling expensive at scale (sole-user JARVIS exempt; multi-user mode would sample at 10%). Alternatives evaluated: Phoenix/Arize OK; LangSmith REJECTED (closed + LangChain coupling); OpenLLMetry OK lower-level. Pick: Langfuse.

Compactor Design Pattern (kb_compact.py reference, reusable for future ChromaDB / GraphRAG / extracted-images compactors): SEVEN reusable primitives. (1) DI'd embedder: Embedder = Callable[[Sequence[str]], np.ndarray]; module never imports sentence-transformers/anthropic — caller wires it (per Decision line 161 layer-boundary discipline). (2) Type-isolated pairwise dedup: same_type_only=True default; a Decision and a Failure on the same topic capture different signals and must both stay; cross-type collapse is dangerous. (3) Structural rule: cosine >= threshold AND tag_jaccard >= overlap_threshold; either alone is insufficient (cosine=0.95 with tag_jaccard=0 = false positive). (4) Quality-aware merge: pick winner by tuple (DIRECTIVE-in-content, content-length, tag-count); ties broken by earliest-timestamp (provenance preservation). Tag-union into winner via merge_tags_into_winner. (5) Transitive redirect resolution: state[line]->current-winner, redirects[loser]->winner, resolve(line) follows chains with cycle break. Handles A->B then B->C correctly. (6) Atomic write: tempfile.mkstemp in same dir + fsync + os.replace (POSIX-atomic rename); .bak preserved before write; on exception unlink tmp + reraise (original KB intact). (7) Default safety: dry-run is default; --force required; audit log appends to compaction_log.jsonl (machine-local, gitignored) every run. SMOKE TESTS via --self-test arg: 6 pure-functional checks (expiry parsing, tag_jaccard math, _pick_winner ordering, provenance tiebreak, tag merge, dedup integration with stub embedder). DIRECTIVE: when building the Stage 4.6 GraphRAG compactor or any future Memory-hygiene tool, port these 7 primitives directly. Do not reinvent. The dedup core is pure-functional and trivially unit-testable; the embedder and KB-IO are the only side-effecting layers.

STEAL #3 (Build Stage 6): Port OpenJarvis MonitorOperativeAgent state-persistence layer (NOT the ReAct loop) to JARVIS Finance/hologram cold-wake agents. Source: OpenJarvis/src/openjarvis/agents/monitor_operative.py:1-613, specifically `_recall_state()` (538-552), `_load_session()` (554-576), `_save_session()` (~330). Mechanism: agent maintains `(operator_id, session_store, memory_backend)`; on tick — loads previous N messages from session_store, recalls state from memory_backend.retrieve(f"monitor_operative:{operator_id}:state"), appends to system prompt as "## Previous State"; after tick — configurable memory extraction (causality_graph / scratchpad / structured_json / none) + observation compression (summarize / truncate / none). JARVIS application: Stage 6 Financier cold-wake pattern (NSE bulk-deals + Groww + news ingestion -> trigger -> cold-wake Kimi+Financier-LoRA on RunPod -> notify, per Decision line 217). DO NOT copy the ReAct loop — Decision line 222 commits OpenClaude as the loop owner; copy ONLY the state-management methods and wrap them with OpenClaude tool-call instead of MonitorOperativeAgent's internal loop. Effort: medium, ~3-5 days at Stage 6 entry. DIRECTIVE: When designing Stage 6 agentic infrastructure, base Finance/hologram cold-wake state layer on this pattern. Replace memory_backend with JARVIS Memory stack (store/expansion/compression/bm25/hybrid/rerank); replace operator_id with specialist_id (e.g., "financier_lora_v1"); use JSONL+ChromaDB for session_store, NOT OpenJarvis's SQLite SessionStore (already rejected in master verdict line 223).

Mechanism: threading.Thread poller with poll_interval, registered tasks with (next_run_time, callback) tuples, EventBus emission on SCHEDULER_TASK_START/END. Simple ~150-line background daemon. NSE market-close trigger -> daemon ticks every N min during market hours -> trigger evaluator (price spikes, bulk-deal anomalies) -> if trigger fires, call cold-wake-Financier(state). Effort: low, ~1-2 days, mostly direct copy with EventBus integration. DIRECTIVE: Pair with STEAL #3 — together they are the agentic infrastructure (cron + ingestion + triggers + cold-wake + notifier) called out as Stage 6 work in Decision line 217. Land both in same Stage 6 sprint.

STEAL #5 (Stage 2.5.7 implementation): Port OpenJarvis TraceStep + StepType enum + event-bus collection pattern to JARVIS Langfuse adapter. Source: OpenJarvis/src/openjarvis/traces/collector.py:14-217 + traces/store.py:80-294. Mechanism: TraceCollector wraps an agent and subscribes to EventBus events (INFERENCE_START, TOOL_CALL_START, MEMORY_RETRIEVE); each step -> TraceStep(step_type, ts, duration, input, output, metadata); final Trace aggregates query+agent_id+model+engine+steps[]+result+messages[]+total_tokens+total_latency_seconds+cost_usd; per-step fields include prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, ttft, energy_joules, gpu_utilization_pct. JARVIS application: Stage 2.5.7 (already on roadmap; Procedural at line 220 plans Langfuse self-host) — extract OpenJarvis's TraceStep dataclass + StepType enum (ROUTE/RETRIEVE/GENERATE/TOOL_CALL/RESPOND), adapt event handler to post to Langfuse instead of SQLite, KEEP the event-bus injection pattern (it's cleaner than patching every agent method individually). Effort: medium, ~2-3 days. DIRECTIVE: When implementing Stage 2.5.7 Langfuse self-host stack (Postgres + ClickHouse + Node server per Procedural line 220), use OpenJarvis's schema as the trace-builder template; do NOT skip the per-step `messages[]` capture (critical for replaying conversations and debugging routing decisions later).

STEAL #6 (Stage 4.A + Stage 5.3): Port OpenJarvis EvalRecord -> EvalResult -> RunSummary framework to JARVIS evaluation harness. Source: OpenJarvis/src/openjarvis/evals/core/{types,runner,scorer}.py. Mechanism: EvalRecord(problem, reference, category, metadata) -> EvalRunner.run() (parallel via ThreadPoolExecutor) -> EvalResult(is_correct, score, latency, cost_usd, energy_joules, ipw, ipj) -> RunSummary with MetricStats computing p50/p95/p99 (NOT just mean — production routing latency tail matters more than mean); pluggable Scorer ABC + LLMJudgeScorer reusable. JARVIS application: Stage 4.A router quality gate (>=80% accuracy on tests/router_eval.jsonl currently uses ad-hoc JSON; replace with structured EvalResult schema and add specialist-specific fields router_accuracy, decomposition_quality, specialist_quality) + Stage 5.3 Engineer evaluation gate (per Decision line 213, gate is 'match Kimi K2.6 base AND outperform on user's private-corpus tasks' — this needs structured scorers, not ad-hoc). Effort: medium, ~3-5 days dataclass refactor + multi-scorer harness. DIRECTIVE: Land before Stage 4.A entry — gate-quality measurement infrastructure must exist before the gate is checkable. Add JARVIS-specific scorer subclasses for our 8 KB types (Episodic/Semantic/Procedural/Idea/Decision/Failure/Cognitive_Pattern/System_Protocol).

Reverses Decisions 177 (2026-05-01) + 222 (2026-05-03) OpenClaude delegation. New direction: build jarvis_core/agent/ from scratch per master roadmap original 5 sub-phases (3.1 Function Calling + outlines, 3.2 Tool Registry, 3.3 Planning, 3.4 ReAct loop, 3.5 MemGPT). Rationale: (1) OpenClaude IS Claude Code runtime hosted by Anthropic; every query routes to Anthropic servers, contradicts 'private AI' identity in JARVIS_ENDGAME.md Section 1; (2) per-token API forever breaks fixed-training-cost + variable-compute economic model from Decision 213/217 (the same economic argument that rejected frontier APIs as the Brain default); (3) 12-specialist routing + QLoRA adapter swap + cold-wake patterns aren't first-class concepts in OpenClaude, would require wrappers that defeat the time-savings the original delegation was reasoned on; (4) Stages 3.1-3.5 in master roadmap are the canonical learning arc; OpenClaude was a 2-3 week detour. Build timeline: 10-14 weeks for Stage 3 deliverable, compressed via OpenJarvis STEAL targets (Apache 2.0 source-copy): STEAL #1 RegistryBase -> 3.2; STEAL #2 PRICING dict -> 3.2 concurrent; STEAL #5 TraceStep+EventBus -> 3.4 trace path; STEAL #6 EvalRecord framework -> 3.4 + 3.5 gates. Net delay vs OpenClaude path: 8-12 weeks on 9-15 month horizon, acceptable. CASCADES: (a) Decision 222 'KILL OpenClaude memory subsystem' directive moot (no longer using OpenClaude as runtime); (b) MCP bridge becomes optional Stage 5+ work for *publishing* JARVIS Memory to external tools (Claude Desktop, Antigravity, third-party agents) — NOT the agent runtime; (c) STAGE_3_OPENCLAUDE_STRATEGY.md to be marked SUPERSEDED 2026-05-13; (d) Master roadmap Stage 3 reverts to original 5 sub-phases (follow-up commit); (e) JARVIS_ANALYSIS.md bear-case 'OpenClaude single-maintainer risk' neutralized; (f) jarvis_core/brain/ note in Decision 177 ('NOT re-implemented in jarvis_core/brain/') reversed — jarvis_core/agent/ becomes the canonical location for ReAct, Plan, Tool, Memory-augmented patterns. DIRECTIVE: Stage 3 entry sprint = jarvis_core/agent/registry.py (STEAL #1) + cost.py (STEAL #2) + tool.py (Tool ABC) as concurrent first landings, before 3.1 Function Calling lesson.

Metacognitive review (jarvis_data/Metacognitive_Daemon_Review.md, 2026-05-09, YELLOW verdict) NOW-phase items absorb INTO Stage 3 sub-phases as the agent framework is built from scratch (per Decision 2026-05-13 reversal of 177/222), NOT as a separate parallel sprint. Integration mapping: (3.1 Function Calling & Structured Output) Cognitive_State_Update Pydantic schema + TextTelemetry dataclass (prompt_brevity, typo_density, correction_rate, rephrasing, sentiment_shift) land alongside other constrained-generation schemas — natural slot because 3.1 already introduces outlines/Pydantic for tool-call typing; (3.4 ReAct Pattern) MIRROR-lite as single-pass system prompt template ('evaluate Goals/Reasoning/Memory before responding') injected into ReAct loop — zero extra inference cost, plus CoT loop detector via regex on <think> tag content (transition tokens: wait/alternatively/re-evaluate, frequency > 2 = flag) in the trace-logging path; (3.5 Memory-Augmented Agents/MemGPT) heartbeat flag on tool calls + async heartbeat event loop + sleep-time consolidation agent = the MemGPT page-out path itself — these aren't additions, they ARE MemGPT. DEFER unchanged from review: Stage 4 (Orchestration) gets full Meta-R1 thinking-token monitor + semantic entropy confidence scoring + veto gate w/ 3s timeout; Stage 5 (Specialists) gets real MIRROR parallel threads via Kimi adapter swap + Quiet-STaR if fine-tuning supports custom thought tokens; Stage 6 (Interface) gets voice telemetry — MFCC, speaker baselines, attention-weighted multimodal fusion. NEW DESIGN CONSTRAINT for Stage 3.5 sleep-time consolidation: heartbeat-emitted Cognitive_Pattern entries MUST carry tag 'heartbeat-emitted'; kb_compact.py MUST exclude entries with that tag from structural-rule displacement (without exclusion, 5 consecutive heartbeats observing 'user in Flow state' cluster together and get pruned as near-duplicates, destroying legitimate transient state observations). A separate stream-compaction process rolls heartbeat entries up per session into one summary (treat like log rotation, not like dedup). Compactor handles insights (Decision/Failure/Cognitive_Pattern stable patterns), stream-compactor handles state observations (heartbeat-emitted Cognitive_Pattern) — different policies, different lifetimes. Effort estimate: Cognitive_State_Update + TextTelemetry schemas ~1 day; MIRROR-lite prompt + CoT regex ~1 day inside 3.4; heartbeat loop + sleep-time consolidation ~1-2 weeks as part of 3.5; kb_compact.py exclusion rule ~2 hours follow-up patch to scripts/kb_compact.py (filter by `'heartbeat-emitted' in entry.tags` before structural-rule pass). DIRECTIVE: when reaching 3.5 implementation, BEFORE writing the heartbeat loop, patch kb_compact.py with the exclusion rule + write a stream-compactor stub so the compaction policy is in place before writes start.
